---
editor:
    render-on-save: true
# bibliography: references.bib
csl: ieee.csl
format:
  html:
    theme:
      light: cosmo
      dark: superhero
---

# Loadings-matrix shrinkage priors for low-rank covariance models {#sec-loadings-shrinkage}

The low-rank covariance parameterization
$$
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} =
\underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{\underline{W}}^{\top}
+ \mathrm{diag}(\underline{d}),
\qquad \underline{\underline{W}} \in \mathbb{R}^{G \times k}
$${#eq-loadings-low-rank-recap}

underlies the LNM, PLN, and NBLN observation models
([@sec-logistic-normal-multinomial], [@sec-poisson-lognormal],
[@sec-nb-lognormal]). The latent dimension $k$ controls how many
regulatory programs the model can represent. In the original
formulations $k$ was a hyperparameter to be picked manually — too
small loses real structure; too large lets the model fit noise. This
section develops a **shrinkage prior on the columns of
$\underline{\underline{W}}$** that turns $k$ into an upper bound and
lets the prior pick the effective rank adaptively. The prior is
implemented as a modular *strategy framework* in
``src/scribe/laplace/_w_priors.py``: each strategy plugs into the
PLN/NBLN Laplace path through a small protocol (initialization,
log-prior, diagnostics), and adding new families (row-wise,
element-wise, etc.) requires one new class plus one registry entry.

## Motivation: the singular-value-shelf failure mode {#sec-loadings-motivation}

At generous $k$ (say $k = 32$) with no shrinkage on
$\underline{\underline{W}}$, the gauge-invariant singular-value
spectrum of
$\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp = \underline{\underline{P}}\,
\underline{\underline{W}}$ (see [@sec-diffexp-nbln-robustness] for
the projection) typically shows a *flat shelf*: the dominant 2–3
factors carry strong signal, but the remaining $k - 3$ factors sit at
~20% of the dominant factor's energy each, contributing roughly
half the cumulative cross-gene compositional covariance. This excess
capacity paints **spurious cross-block diagonal contours** in
compositional corner PPCs: model density extends in coordinated
directions that the empirical scatter does not support.

Manually capping $k$ (e.g. $k = 5$) fixes the symptom but is not
portable across datasets — different tissues and assays support
different effective ranks. The principled fix is to keep $k$
generous and let a **shrinkage prior on the columns of
$\underline{\underline{W}}$** drive unused factors toward zero. The
prior pays no penalty for keeping data-supported factors at their
natural scale and pays a heavy penalty for filling unused factors
with noise; the resulting MAP is approximately rank-sparse on the
column index.

## Where the prior acts: $\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp$, not raw $\underline{\underline{W}}$ {#sec-loadings-projection}

For PLN/NBLN, raw $\underline{\underline{W}}$ has a rigid-translation
gauge component along the all-ones direction (Theorem 2 in
[@sec-diffexp-nbln-robustness]): writing
$\underline{\underline{W}} = \underline{\underline{W}}_\perp +
\underline{\underline{W}}_{\parallel}$ with
$\underline{\underline{W}}_{\parallel} = \mathbf{1}\,\mathbf{u}^{\top}$
for some $\mathbf{u} \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$, only the gene-centered
projection $\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp$ carries
biologically meaningful cross-gene signal; the
$\underline{\underline{W}}_{\parallel}$ component is a per-cell
scaling slop coupled to $\underline{\eta}$.

Applying shrinkage to raw $\underline{\underline{W}}$ would dissipate
prior mass on the gauge component as well as the biological one. The
observation-model integration layer in SCRIBE therefore projects
$\underline{\underline{W}} \to \underline{\underline{W}}_\perp$ at
the strategy boundary:

```python
W_for_prior = W - mean(W, axis=0)            # gauge-cleaned
log_prior = strategy.log_prior(W_for_prior, aux, n_constraints=1)
```

The flag ``n_constraints=1`` declares that each column of
``W_for_prior`` lives in a $(G-1)$-dimensional subspace
(centered columns sum to zero). The strategy itself stays
*model-agnostic*: it accepts whatever ``W_for_prior`` is handed in
and uses ``n_constraints`` to scale the Gaussian normalizer
correctly. For LNM-family models (where $\underline{\underline{W}}$
already lives in ALR-quotient coordinates and the
all-ones-gauge does not exist) the obs model would pass raw
$\underline{\underline{W}}$ with ``n_constraints=0`` — same strategy
code, different boundary treatment.

## Strategy protocol {#sec-loadings-protocol}

Each shrinkage strategy declares three pieces of information and
implements three methods:

- ``type_name``: registry key (e.g. ``"horseshoe_columnwise"``).
- ``aux_param_names``: tuple of optimizer-state parameter names
  this strategy adds (e.g. ``("w_raw_lambda_k", "w_raw_tau")`` for
  horseshoe). Names are ``w_``-prefixed to namespace them within the
  optax params dict.
- ``init_aux_params(G, k, rng_key) -> dict``: initial unconstrained
  values for the aux parameters.
- ``log_prior(W_for_prior, aux, *, n_constraints=0) -> scalar``:
  the log-prior contribution added to the outer ELBO at every
  optimization step. Includes Jacobian terms for any
  constrained-positive aux parameters.
- ``diagnostics(W_for_prior, aux, *, n_constraints=0) -> dict``:
  post-fit interpretable quantities reported on
  ``result.w_prior_diagnostics``.

The user-facing API routes through the canonical ``priors`` dict:

```python
scribe.fit(
    adata, model="nbln", inference_method="laplace",
    priors={
        "capture_efficiency": (np.log(M_0), sigma_M),
        "loadings": {"type": "horseshoe_columnwise", "tau_scale": 1.0},
    },
    latent_dim=16,
)
```

The descriptive key ``"loadings"`` is the factor-analysis term for
$\underline{\underline{W}}$ and aliases the internal key ``"W"``.

## The three column-wise strategies {#sec-loadings-strategies}

The v1 implementation ships three column-wise strategies (per-factor
scales). All math fixes documented below apply identically to each.

### Gaussian (ridge) — ``"gaussian"`` {#sec-loadings-gaussian}

Shared fixed scale:
$$
\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp[:, k] \sim
\mathcal{N}\!\left(\underline{0},\; \sigma^{2}\,
\underline{\underline{I}}_{G}\right),
\qquad k = 1, \ldots, K,
$${#eq-loadings-gaussian}

with $\sigma$ a fixed hyperparameter. No auxiliary parameters. The
sanity-check baseline: tells you whether the symptom is "any
shrinkage helps" or "specifically *sparsity* helps" — if Gaussian
gives the right answer, you do not need the heavier machinery.

### Column-wise horseshoe — ``"horseshoe_columnwise"`` {#sec-loadings-horseshoe}

Local-global hierarchy with **standard-deviation** local scales:
$$
\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp[:, k] \mid \lambda_{k} \sim
\mathcal{N}\!\left(\underline{0},\; \lambda_{k}\,
\underline{\underline{I}}_{G}\right),
\qquad
\lambda_{k} \mid \tau \sim \mathrm{HalfCauchy}(\tau),
\qquad
\tau \sim \mathrm{HalfCauchy}(\tau_{0}).
$${#eq-loadings-horseshoe}

Aux parameters: $\mathrm{raw}_{\lambda_{k}}$, $\mathrm{raw}_{\tau}$ —
the softplus-floor parameterization detailed in
[@sec-loadings-softplus]. Recommended default for cascade-frozen
NBLN fits: horseshoe combines a heavy tail (lets strong factors
through) with a sharp peak at zero (kills unused factors).

### Column-wise NEG — ``"neg_columnwise"`` {#sec-loadings-neg}

Normal-Exponential-Gamma hierarchy with **variance** local scales:
$$
\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp[:, k] \mid \psi_{k} \sim
\mathcal{N}\!\left(\underline{0},\; \sqrt{\psi_{k}}\,
\underline{\underline{I}}_{G}\right),
\qquad
\psi_{k} \mid \gamma \sim \mathrm{Exponential}(\mathrm{rate}=\gamma),
\qquad
\gamma \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta).
$${#eq-loadings-neg}

More aggressive near-zero shrinkage than horseshoe at default
hyperparameters; useful when horseshoe is insufficient to kill
noise factors on a particular dataset.

## Math fixes that matter {#sec-loadings-math-fixes}

Three subtle implementation details have to be right or the prior
runs but silently produces a wrong fit.

### Standard deviation vs variance {#sec-loadings-std-vs-variance}

The NumPyro ``Normal(loc, scale)`` parameterization uses **scale =
standard deviation**. The horseshoe pseudocode above writes
$\mathcal{N}(\underline{0}, \lambda_{k})$ where $\lambda_{k}$ is
explicitly the std (variance is $\lambda_{k}^{2}$); the NEG
pseudocode writes $\mathcal{N}(\underline{0}, \sqrt{\psi_{k}})$
where $\psi_{k}$ is explicitly the variance.

Conflating these — writing $\mathcal{N}(\underline{0},
\lambda_{k}^{2})$ for horseshoe or $\mathcal{N}(\underline{0},
\psi_{k})$ for NEG — produces a fit that runs but converges to the
wrong $\lambda_{k}$ / $\psi_{k}$ by a factor of two in log-units.
The unit tests in ``tests/test_w_priors.py`` include explicit
numerical checks: for horseshoe with $\lambda_{k} = 2$ and
$\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp = 1$, the Normal log-density
should match $\log \mathcal{N}(1; 0, 2) \approx -1.74$, **not**
$\log \mathcal{N}(1; 0, 4) \approx -2.61$.

### Softplus-floor reparameterization {#sec-loadings-softplus}

A naïve log-space parameterization
$\lambda_{k} = \exp(\log \lambda_{k})$ combined with the Gaussian
likelihood produces an *unbounded above* log-prior as
$\lambda_{k} \to 0$. The dominant terms are
$$
\log p(\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp[:, k] \mid \lambda_{k}) =
- \tfrac{1}{2} \| \underline{\underline{W}}_\perp[:, k] \|^{2}\,
\lambda_{k}^{-2}
- (G - 1) \log \lambda_{k}
+ \text{const},
$${#eq-loadings-collapse-likelihood}

and the change-of-variables Jacobian for $\log \lambda_{k}$
contributes $+\log \lambda_{k}$. Their sum, on the
$\log \lambda_{k}$-axis, is
$-(G - 1) \log \lambda_{k} + \log \lambda_{k} =
-(G - 2) \log \lambda_{k}$, which **diverges to $+\infty$** as
$\log \lambda_{k} \to -\infty$ for $G > 2$. The negative-log-likelihood
loss therefore diverges to $-\infty$ along the ridge
$\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp \to \underline{0},\,
\lambda_{k} \to 0$ — the optimizer collapses to that degenerate MAP
in just a few steps. (NEG has the same pathology for $G > 4$ via
$-\tfrac{1}{2}(G - 1) \log \psi_{k}$ minus the Jacobian.)

The fix is the **softplus-floor reparameterization**:
$$
\lambda_{k} = \lambda_{\min} + \mathrm{softplus}(\mathrm{raw}_{\lambda_{k}}),
\qquad
\mathrm{raw}_{\lambda_{k}} \in \mathbb{R},
\qquad
\lambda_{\min} > 0.
$${#eq-loadings-softplus}

Default $\lambda_{\min} = 10^{-3}$ for horseshoe and
$\psi_{\min} = 10^{-6}$ for NEG (variance scale). The Jacobian for
$\mathrm{raw}_{\lambda_{k}} \to \lambda_{k}$ is
$\sigma(\mathrm{raw}_{\lambda_{k}})$ where $\sigma$ is the logistic
sigmoid, so the log-Jacobian is
$\log \sigma(\mathrm{raw}_{\lambda_{k}})$ — **bounded above by 0**
and tending to $-\infty$ as $\mathrm{raw}_{\lambda_{k}} \to -\infty$.
The Jacobian therefore actively *prevents* the optimizer from
collapsing $\lambda_{k}$ toward zero; any savings in
$-\log p(\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp[:, k] \mid \lambda_{k})$
from a smaller $\lambda_{k}$ are paid back through the bounded
Jacobian.

A subtle point: this is **log-scale constrained MAP**, not the MCMC
non-centered parameterization (NCP). True NCP would optimize a
separate $\underline{\underline{Z}}$ with
$\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp[:, k] = \lambda_{k}\,
\underline{\underline{Z}}[:, k]$; we optimize
$\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp$ directly with $\lambda_{k}$ as a
constrained scale parameter. The two parameterizations both block
the collapse-to-zero singularity, but NCP introduces a different
optimization ridge along $\lambda \cdot Z = \mathrm{const}$ that we
prefer to avoid.

### Subspace correction {#sec-loadings-subspace}

When the obs model passes $\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp$ to the
strategy (as it does for PLN/NBLN), each column of
$\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp$ satisfies the constraint
$\sum_{g=1}^{G} W_{\perp,gk} = 0$ — every column lives in a
$(G - 1)$-dimensional subspace of $\mathbb{R}^{G}$, not all of
$\mathbb{R}^{G}$.

The centered-column Gaussian log-density must therefore use the
**effective dimension**
$d_{\mathrm{eff}} = G - n_{\mathrm{constraints}}$ in its normalizer:
$$
\log p(\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp[:, k]) =
- \tfrac{1}{2}\, \frac{\| \underline{\underline{W}}_\perp[:, k] \|^{2}}{\lambda_{k}^{2}}
- d_{\mathrm{eff}}\, \log \lambda_{k}
- \tfrac{1}{2}\, d_{\mathrm{eff}}\, \log(2\pi).
$${#eq-loadings-subspace-density}

The obs model passes ``n_constraints=1`` for centered columns; LNM
or raw-W debugging paths pass ``0``. Calling
``dist.Normal(0.0, lambda_k).log_prob(W_perp).sum()`` naïvely uses
$d_{\mathrm{eff}} = G$ in the normalizer and over-counts the
$-\log \lambda_{k}$ term by 1. The bias on the optimal
$\lambda_{k}$ is a factor of
$\sqrt{G / (G - 1)} \approx 1 + \tfrac{1}{2G}$ — negligible at
$G \sim 10^{4}$ (real scRNA-seq) but material on small synthetic
benchmarks at $G \sim 10$. Writing the centered-column density
manually with the correct $d_{\mathrm{eff}}$ keeps the math right
across both scales.

## Diagnostics: headline rank from $\| \underline{\underline{W}}_\perp[:, k] \|$, not $\sigma_{k}$ {#sec-loadings-diagnostics}

The strategy's ``diagnostics`` method reports a primary
*column-norm-based* rank and a secondary *scale-based* rank:

- ``column_frobenius_compositional`` $= \| \underline{\underline{W}}_\perp[:, k] \|$ per factor.
- ``column_norm_effective_rank`` = number of factors whose column
  norm exceeds 5% of the dominant factor's norm. **Headline
  diagnostic.** Also exposed under the alias ``effective_rank`` for
  terse client code.
- ``sigma_k`` = per-column scales from the aux MAP
  ($\lambda_{k}$ for horseshoe, $\sqrt{\psi_{k}}$ for NEG).
- ``scale_effective_rank`` = same threshold applied to ``sigma_k``.
  Secondary diagnostic.

The headline anchors on the column norm because **only the column
norm directly enters
$\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp\, \underline{\underline{W}}_\perp^{\top}$**
and hence the compositional covariance that downstream
correlation analyses and PPCs visualize. Aux scales can be weakly
identified under heavy-tailed priors — a horseshoe fit can drive
$\lambda_{k}$ to its softplus floor while the data still supports a
small but non-zero column norm in that direction. Reporting both
diagnostics surfaces the disagreement when it happens.

The companion visualization
``scribe.viz.plot_w_shrinkage_spectrum`` plots
$\| \underline{\underline{W}}_\perp[:, k] \|$ on a log-scale primary
axis with $\sigma_{k}$ as an optional dashed-secondary overlay; the
threshold for ``column_norm_effective_rank`` is drawn as a
horizontal line.

## Calibration workflow {#sec-loadings-calibration}

The W-prior log-density enters the loss **unscaled** while the
likelihood scales as $\mathcal{O}(N_{\mathrm{cells}})$. The prior's
*effective strength* therefore scales inversely with dataset size —
the same $\tau_{0}$ that produces a clean elbow on a 10k-cell
dataset may produce no visible shrinkage on a 100k-cell dataset.
Re-tune hyperparameters when transferring across datasets; as a
rule of thumb, multiply $\tau_{0}$ by
$\sqrt{N_{\mathrm{old}} / N_{\mathrm{new}}}$ for cross-dataset
transfer.

The 5-step calibration recipe:

1. **Fit with the default** ($\tau_{0} = 1.0$) and a generous
   ``latent_dim`` (e.g. 16).
2. **Inspect ``column_norm_effective_rank``**:
   - If it equals ``latent_dim``: no shrinkage is happening —
     tighten by reducing $\tau_{0}$ by 10x.
   - If it equals 1: over-shrunk — loosen by 10x.
   - If it falls in $[2, \texttt{latent\_dim}/2]$: keep.
3. **Inspect the spectrum plot.** A clean fit shows a sharp elbow
   at the effective rank with factors beyond collapsing to
   near-zero.
4. **Sanity-check the aux-scale floor.** Confirm
   ``sigma_k.min()`` is well above $\lambda_{\min}$ for active
   factors. A handful of "dead" factors touching the floor is
   expected and OK.
5. **Re-run the compositional corner PPC.** Spurious diagonal
   contours should collapse to data-consistent ones.

## Compatibility and scope {#sec-loadings-scope}

The v1 implementation:

- **Targets PLN and NBLN Laplace fits.** Engine raises
  ``NotImplementedError`` for ``model="lnm"`` or ``"lnmvcp"``
  with a non-``"none"`` ``w_prior`` config — ALR-space W has
  different shrinkage semantics that need a separate design pass.
- **Combines cleanly with Phase-2 cascade freeze.** The shrinkage
  strategy is orthogonal to ``informative_priors_freeze`` — freeze
  pins $\underline{r}$ and $\underline{\eta}$ at cascade values;
  shrinkage regularizes $\underline{\underline{W}}$. Both
  mechanisms run together by default for cascade-frozen NBLN fits
  with the loadings prior active.
- **Leaves the gauge component unregularized by ``w_prior``.** The
  prior targets $\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp$ only; the
  all-ones component of raw $\underline{\underline{W}}$ is pinned
  structurally by the cascade freeze on $\underline{\eta}$, not by
  the shrinkage prior. Users monitor drift via
  ``get_gauge_diagnostics()`` — if
  ``gauge_contamination_ratio > 0.2``, the upstream fix is to
  re-examine the NBVCP source / capture prior, not to add a
  ridge on the gauge component.

## Extension roadmap {#sec-loadings-roadmap}

The strategy protocol is W-shape-agnostic and accommodates future
families with one new class and one registry entry. Three
extensions are likely candidates:

- **Row-wise shrinkage.** Per-gene scales
  $\sigma_{g} \sim \mathrm{HalfCauchy}(\tau)$ with
  $\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp[g, :] \mid \sigma_{g} \sim
  \mathcal{N}(\underline{0}, \sigma_{g}^{2}\,
  \underline{\underline{I}}_{k})$. Useful when sparsity is
  expected per-gene (most genes load on nothing, a few load on
  multiple factors) rather than per-factor.
- **Element-wise shrinkage.** Per-(gene, factor) scales
  $\sigma_{gk}$. Maximum flexibility but $G \times k$ extra
  parameters — typically only justified when the data has
  enough cells to identify them.
- **LNM-family compatibility.** ALR-space
  $\underline{\underline{W}}$ has shape $(G-1) \times k$ and lives
  in a gauge-fixed quotient space, so the projection step
  ([@sec-loadings-projection]) is a no-op. Adding LNM support is
  one ``elif`` branch in the engine plus the obs-model
  integration pattern; no new strategy code.

## Summary {#sec-loadings-summary}

The loadings-matrix shrinkage prior turns the latent dimension from
a hyperparameter the user must guess into an upper bound the data
selects. The v1 framework ships three column-wise strategies
(Gaussian, horseshoe, NEG) accessed through the standard ``priors``
dict under the descriptive key ``"loadings"``. Three implementation
details (std-vs-variance, softplus-floor against the scale-collapse
singularity, subspace correction for the $(G-1)$-dimensional
centered-column subspace) keep the math right at any data size.
The headline diagnostic ``column_norm_effective_rank`` anchors on
the data-supported column norm of
$\underline{\underline{W}}_\perp$ rather than the aux MAP
$\sigma_{k}$, which can be weakly identified under heavy-tailed
priors. The companion visualization
``scribe.viz.plot_w_shrinkage_spectrum`` renders the elbow plot for
inspection. The framework combines orthogonally with the Phase-2
cascade freeze on $\underline{r}$ and $\underline{\eta}$
([@sec-nbln-cascade-freeze]) and is the recommended setup for
modern NBLN Laplace fits at generous ``latent_dim``.
